title: Jeremiah 28
subtitle: Hananiah Breaks the Yoke

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Hananiah Breaks the Yoke

heb.paragraph: Hananiah breaks (shabar) the yoke from Jeremiah's neck (v.10) and proclaims: "In the same way I will break the yoke of Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon off the neck of all the nations." The physical act is a counter-sign-act -- a false prophet performing his own symbolic action to contradict the true prophet. The verb shabar echoes the broken flask of chapter 19, but here the breaking is itself an act of prophetic rebellion.

refs.note: The test of true prophecy: "If what a prophet proclaims does not come about, that is a message the LORD has not spoken." Jeremiah invokes this criterion.
Zedekiah son of Kenaanah strikes Micaiah the prophet -- the same pattern of a false prophet using physical force against a true one.

notes.note: Hananiah's prophecy: two years, vessels returned, Jehoiachin restored. The specificity ("two years") makes the prophecy testable -- and falsifiable.
"Amen! May the LORD do so!" -- Jeremiah's response is not sarcasm but genuine hope tempered by prophetic realism. He wants peace to be true but knows it is not.

The peace-prophet test: only fulfilled prophecy validates a peace oracle. Judgment prophets have an inherent advantage -- the prophetic tradition is weighted toward warning, and peace claims require proof.
Hananiah breaks the wooden yoke. Jeremiah says nothing and walks away. The silence is devastating -- the true prophet does not argue but waits for God to respond.

Calvin reads Jeremiah's "Amen" as a mark of humility: "A true prophet does not relish announcing judgment. He would prefer peace -- but he cannot proclaim what God has not spoken.". Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: "The fifth month of the fourth year" of Zedekiah = 594 BC. This is shortly after the diplomatic summit of chapter 27 -- Hananiah's prophecy directly contradicts Jeremiah's yoke message. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

lundbom.source: Jack R. Lundbom, Jeremiah, Anchor Bible (3 vols., 1999–2004) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Lundbom identifies this as the climactic prophet-vs-prophet confrontation in the book. Two prophets, both claiming divine authority, deliver contradictory messages in the same temple court. The audience has no way to distinguish between them except by waiting for outcomes.

brueggemann.source: Walter Brueggemann, Jeremiah, ITC (1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Brueggemann reads Jeremiah's silence as "the most powerful prophetic act in the chapter." The true prophet does not compete with the false one in the public arena. He withdraws, trusts God to vindicate, and waits. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

hist.context: Hananiah son of Azzur prophesies that within two years the temple vessels will be returned and Jehoiachin will come home. His message is everything the people want to hear. Jeremiah initially responds with cautious hope: "Amen! May the LORD do so!" (v.6). But he adds the critical test: "The prophet who prophesies peace will be recognized as one truly sent by the LORD only if his prediction comes true" (v.9). Hananiah then physically breaks the yoke off Jeremiah's neck. Jeremiah walks away.

sections.header: Verses 12-17 -- The Iron Yoke and Hananiah's Death

heb.paragraph: God replaces the broken wooden yoke with one of iron (barzel, v.13). The escalation is devastating: Hananiah's act of defiance has not removed the burden but made it heavier. Wooden yokes can break; iron yokes cannot. By resisting God's word, Hananiah has increased the severity of judgment.

refs.note: "An iron yoke on your neck" -- the covenant curse language that God invokes through the iron yoke replacement.
Ananias and Sapphira die for lying to the Holy Spirit -- the NT parallel of immediate divine judgment for speaking falsely in God's name.

notes.note: The wooden-to-iron escalation: resistance to judgment produces heavier judgment. The false comfort Hananiah offered has made things worse, not better.

"The LORD has not sent you, yet you have persuaded this nation to trust in lies." The charge is not merely false prophecy but rebellion (sara) against the LORD -- the most serious offense in prophetic law.
"In the seventh month of that same year, Hananiah the prophet died." The prophecy of death was fulfilled within two months. This validates Jeremiah's word over Hananiah's.

Calvin draws the universal lesson: "When people reject God's lighter discipline, they receive heavier. The wooden yoke of submission becomes the iron yoke of forced servitude. Better to bow willingly than to be broken unwillingly.". Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The verb sara ("rebellion") is used specifically for inciting revolt against God or an established authority (cf. Deut 13:5, Jer 29:32). Hananiah's false prophecy is classified as sedition against divine governance. The NET Bible's textual notes draw attention to variant readings and translation challenges in this passage. The translators weigh manuscript evidence alongside syntactic analysis to arrive at renderings that balance accuracy with readability, providing supplementary notes where the original resists simple translation.

lundbom.source: Jack R. Lundbom, Jeremiah, Anchor Bible (3 vols., 1999–2004) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Lundbom reads the iron yoke as "prophetic escalation theology." When God's merciful warnings are refused, the next phase is not retreat but intensification. Hananiah's death confirms Jeremiah and permanently discredits the peace faction. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

brueggemann.source: Walter Brueggemann, Jeremiah, ITC (1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Brueggemann calls Hananiah's death "the vindication of prophetic patience." Jeremiah did not need to argue, shout, or compete. Time itself rendered the verdict. This observation invites further reflection on the passage's contribution to the book's theological argument. The text operates on multiple levels simultaneously — historical, literary, and theological — and each level enriches the others when the passage is read with attention to its canonical context.

hist.context: God's response to Hananiah comes through Jeremiah: "You have broken a wooden yoke but have made an iron yoke instead" (v.13). Resistance to divine judgment does not remove it but intensifies it. The death sentence follows: "This very year you are going to die, because you have preached rebellion against the LORD" (v.16). Hananiah dies two months later (v.17).

rows.label: vv.10-11
rows.text: The Yoke Broken: Jeremiah Walks Away

lit.note: The most dramatic prophet-vs-prophet confrontation in the Hebrew Bible. Wooden yoke broken, iron yoke imposed, and the false prophet dies -- all within months.

themes.note: Prophecy peaks at 10 -- this is the definitive test of true vs. false prophecy. Judgment at 9: the iron yoke and Hananiah's death.

hebtext.note: Hananiah breaks (shabar) the yoke from Jeremiah's neck (v.10) and proclaims: "In the same way I will break the yoke of Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon off the neck of all the nations." The physical act is a counter-sign-act -- a false prophet performing his own symbolic action to contradict the true prophet. The verb shabar echoes the broken flask of chapter 19, but here the breaking is itself an act of prophetic rebellion.

God replaces the broken wooden yoke with one of iron (barzel, v.13). The escalation is devastating: Hananiah's act of defiance has not removed the burden but made it heavier. Wooden yokes can break; iron yokes cannot. By resisting God's word, Hananiah has increased the severity of judgment.

trans.title: Comparing translations in Hananiah Breaks the Yoke

translations.text: He said, “Amen! May the Lord do so! May the Lord fulfill the words you have prophesied by bringing the articles of the Lord’s house and all the exiles back to this place from Babylon.

and the prophet Jeremiah said, “Amen! May the LORD do so; may the LORD make the words that you have prophesied come true, and bring back to this place from Babylon the vessels of the house of the LORD, and all the exiles.
But the prophet who prophesies peace will be recognized as one truly sent by the Lord only if his prediction comes true.”
As for the prophet who prophesies peace, when the word of that prophet comes to pass, then it will be known that the LORD has truly sent the prophet.”

Emphasises historical-critical, literary, or text-critical analysis.
Reads Hananiah Breaks the Yoke within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.

thread.text: The test of true prophecy: "If what a prophet proclaims does not come about, that is a message the LORD has not spoken." Jeremiah invokes this criterion.
Zedekiah son of Kenaanah strikes Micaiah the prophet -- the same pattern of a false prophet using physical force against a true one.
"An iron yoke on your neck" -- the covenant curse language that God invokes through the iron yoke replacement.
Ananias and Sapphira die for lying to the Holy Spirit -- the NT parallel of immediate divine judgment for speaking falsely in God's name.

coaching.tip: Hananiah breaks the wooden yoke off Jeremiah's neck (v. 10) — a dramatic counter-sign-act. Jeremiah's initial response is striking: 'Amen! May the LORD do so!' (v. 6). He wants Hananiah to be right. The true prophet doesn't enjoy delivering bad news. Jeremiah wishes his own message were wrong. That vulnerability distinguishes him from a mere contrarian.

God replaces the wooden yoke with an iron one (v. 13) — Hananiah's defiance intensified the judgment rather than averting it. Resisting God's discipline doesn't remove it; it hardens it. Hananiah dies within the year (v. 17), exactly as Jeremiah predicted. The confrontation becomes a case study: opposing the prophetic word doesn't change the future — it just makes the prophet's credibility sharper.

prayer_prompt: As Hananiah breaks the wooden yoke and promises peace, only to die for prophesying lies, consider asking God whether you prefer teachers who confirm what you already want to believe over those who tell you what is true.
